metadata.title: PRIORITY LIST OF ADVERSE EVENTS OF SPECIAL INTEREST FOR EBOLA
metadata.description:
The SPEAC Executive Board welcomes input on this AESI list and will update it periodically. Please share input with the SPEAC and Brighton Collaboration team at bc-coordinator@taskforce.org.

This list of potential Adverse Events of Special Interest (AESI) that could follow vaccines to prevent Ebola was developed based on criteria established by the Safety Platform for Emergency Vaccines (SPEAC), which include:  

  1. A known association with immunization or a specific vaccine platform. 
  2. The occurrence during wild-type disease due to viral replication and/or immunopathogenesis. 
  3. A theoretical association derived from animal models.  

Special populations, including pregnant women, children, adolescents, and immunosuppressed individuals, may experience an increased frequency and severity of any of the AESIs listed as relevant to all populations. There are also specific AESI that are of particular relevance to special populations, and these are listed according to the population. It is important to note that children, pregnant women and immunocompromised individuals may also have increased severity of expected local and systemic reactogenicity, as well as unexpected autoimmune conditions.  

This list is based on available data for Ebola vaccines, for consideration in deployment of investigational vaccines in response to the 2026 epidemic of Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD) in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda. Adaptions may be needed depending on the application. 

Note: This AESI list is meant to be a reference; if certain AESIs on the list will be captured in routine safety surveillance in a study, those AESIs may not need to be protocol-specified. Only a subset of the AESI list will typically need to be included as protocol-specified AESIs: those that would require a systematic approach to data collection to characterize; or that may not be captured in routine safety surveillance.

metadata.title: Finite Screen Spacetime: Entropy-Response Geometry from Causal-Screen Ledgers
metadata.description:
FDS--G1 Complete Series v1.2 presents a finite-screen entropy-response framework for gravity and late-time dark-sector residuals. The series develops Finite Screen Spacetime as a bridge program in which the primitive gravitational object is a finite causal-screen entropy ledger, and geometry is treated as the integrable hydrodynamic response envelope of that ledger.

This v1.2 release is a full body-integrated synchronization release. Its main increment is the integration of the theory-hardening architecture across the Core, Companion, and D-series papers, together with the completed medium-prior 8-seed production-refined nested-evidence audit and the finalized KiDS BandPower EE+nE diagnostic bridge. It does not claim final cosmological confirmation, full survey-grade validation, a completed full 3×2pt result, or a completed microscopic derivation of the finite-screen realization.

The archive includes the Core paper, Companions A--F, the G1DE-M3/4 / G1fit-real data-confrontation paper, and the D0--D10 dark-sector closure sequence. Together these papers formulate the finite-screen response chain, screen statistical mechanics, stochastic screen transition dynamics, scalar-capacity EFT interface, likelihood-ready falsification tests, horizon occupancy residuals, holographic dictionaries, modular anomaly-inflow calibration targets, finite-access sheaf geometry, the 3/4 projection law, non-equilibrium screen entropy, the screen influence functional, Weyl-normalized Ward completion, maximum-caliber memory quotient, finite Markov-screen realization, metric-envelope reconstruction, obstruction exact-sequence classification, and the minimal Weyl-branch bootstrap behind G1DE-M3/4.

The current leading empirical dark-sector branch is the projection-locked G1DE-M3/4 model. Its locked late-time residual pattern is:

s < 3,   μ(a,k) = 1,   Σ(a,k) − 1 = −(3/4)(3 − s) RbH(a),   RbH(1) = 1.

Here RbH(a), also denoted Xb(a) in theory/code-facing notation, is the production-normalized horizon-response output shape. It is not assumed to be a uniquely identified microscopic source--kernel factorization. D6 formalizes this output/lift distinction through a memory quotient and maximum-caliber horizontal lift, while D7 gives a finite-state detailed-balance Markov-screen realization prototype for an admissible passive lift.

A completed medium-prior 8-seed production-refined nested-evidence audit gives the canonical evidence hierarchy:

M3/4 > Mκ > const-Σ > G1DE-2 > G1DE-1 > CPL > ΛCDM.

All seven models share the same medium-prior configuration and eight independent seeds with dlogZ = 0.1. In this production-refined audit, the locked M3/4 branch exceeds the free-κ control by Δlog Z = 0.856 (B ≃ 2.35), constant-Σ by Δlog Z = 1.775 (B ≃ 5.90), G1DE-2 by Δlog Z = 6.523 (B ≃ 6.8 × 102), the growth-only negative control G1DE-1 by Δlog Z = 7.402 (B ≃ 1.65 × 103), CPL by Δlog Z = 9.603 (B ≃ 1.5 × 104), and ΛCDM by Δlog Z = 11.884 (B ≃ 1.45 × 105).

The earlier mixed-provenance raw audit and the earlier three-seed exact-pilot audit are retained only for provenance. They have been superseded for model ranking by the medium-prior 8-seed production-refined table reported in this release.

The v1.2 theory-hardening layer integrates explicitly marked modules for finite-ledger representation, causal-measurement protocol, retarded finite-response kernels, modular-response targets, entropy-admissibility filters, and mainstream μ/Σ production-interface comparisons. Each new theoretical item is marked by claim type: definition, admissibility rule, conditional theorem target, open lemma, empirical protocol, or falsification / demotion rule. These additions harden the architecture but do not complete the microscopic derivation of the screen ledger, the D0 finite-index / boost modular calibration route, the modular derivation of the 3/4 coefficient, or the unique source--kernel realization of RbH(a).

v1.2 also records the completed KiDS BandPower EE+nE diagnostic bridge. This diagnostic track validates the compressed-space KiDS BandPower product/covariance/order bridge for the EE+nE subspace, including BandPower PeeE projector validation, PneE density-kernel smoke tests, local diagnostic refits, deterministic no-noise mock recovery, noisy mock screening, nuisance-prior robustness, prior-regularized refits, and toy nn-closure diagnostics. These results support the interpretation that the EE+nE amplitude degeneracy is driven by the missing clustering channel, but they remain diagnostic only.

The KiDS BandPower track is not a completed full 3×2pt likelihood, not production evidence, and not a final cosmological constraint. Phase 5 sourcing audits found that a full 300×300 BandPower covariance exists and that its first 200×200 block matches the validated EE+nE covariance, but no usable real Pnn / clustering data vector is locally available in the required BandPower format. Therefore full 3×2pt validation remains blocked pending a real nn/Pnn clustering vector or catalog-level recomputation.

The accompanying replication kit has been upgraded to v1.2. It adds production_evidence_v1_2/ with the production-refined nested-evidence runners, configs, per-seed JSON outputs, generated Table 3 CSV, summary files, manifests, checksums, and validation scripts. It also adds kids_bandpower_eene_v1_2/ with KiDS BandPower EE+nE diagnostic scripts, configs, output summaries, data-manifest notes, and claim-boundary documentation, plus phase5_nn_sourcing/ documenting why the full 3×2pt result remains blocked. The v1.1 replication-kit contents are retained for provenance; the v1.2 production-refined audit supersedes the older three-seed audit for current model ranking.

The purpose of this release is to provide a timestamped, internally organized, falsifiable research archive for the FDS--G1 finite-screen gravity program and the G1DE-M3/4 dark-sector branch. The framework is conditional on stated bridge assumptions and includes explicit demotion criteria: failure of the 3/4 projection lock, decisive production-level preference for constant-Σ or binned-Σ(z), unsuppressed μ-channel response, requirement of a free A(a,k), systematic mock-injection false positives, or loss to CPL / ΛCDM under production-level evidence would weaken or demote the relevant branch.

D8--D10 extend the earlier D0--D7 closure sequence without replacing it. D8 reconstructs a universal metric envelope from finite causal access and entropy-density data; D9 classifies admissible residuals through an obstruction exact sequence and no-free-dark-sector rule; D10 proves the minimal Weyl-branch structure behind the projection-locked G1DE-M3/4 model under finite-description, no-free-A(a,k), Ward-suppressed Ricci leakage, passive memory-shape, and isotropic optical-compliance assumptions. The production-refined evidence result supports the data-facing output branch; it does not prove the microscopic screen realization or production-confirm the unique RbH(a) source shape.

Recommended entry points are: Core for the finite-screen entropy-response architecture, G1DE-M3/4 / G1fit-real for the production-refined evidence comparison, Companion D for likelihood-ready falsification and evidence-layer stratification, Companion E for horizon occupancy residuals, D0 for the modular calibration route, D2 for the 3/4 projection law, D5 for Weyl-normalized production and identifiability rules, D6 for memory-output identifiability, D7 for the finite Markov-screen realization prototype, D8--D10 for the reconstruction, obstruction, and minimal Weyl-branch bootstrap layer, and Section 20 for the KiDS BandPower compressed-space bridge and full 3×2pt roadmap.

metadata.title: PoC 3 - Synchronized USV Hydrophone Dataset for Ocean Noise Pollution Monitoring (Trials 1 & 2)
metadata.description:
OpenSwarm PoC 3: Synchronized USV Hydrophone Dataset for Ocean Noise Pollution Monitoring (Trials 1 & 2)

Abstract

This dataset contains acoustic, spatial, and synchronization data collected during Trial 1 (2026.03.04). and Trial 2 (2026.04.20) of the OpenSwarm European Project, specifically supporting Proof of Concept (PoC) 3: Ocean Noise Pollution Monitoring.

The primary objective of this dataset is to provide highly synchronized multi-node acoustic recordings of marine environments to evaluate noise pollution. The setup features a distributed network of Unmanned Surface Vehicles (USVs) configured as sensory buoys, alongside a mobile ground-truth sound source. A critical feature of this dataset is the implementation of a wireless local synchronization system across all nodes, ensuring a common timestamp reference for precise spatiotemporal analysis of the acoustic data.

Description of the Files

  • Trial 1: 2026.03.04
    • 2026_03_04_datasets.zip: dataset
    • 2026_03_04_drone_dji_top_view.mp4: video from the drone camera at the top of the experiment
  • Trial 2: 2026.04.20
    • 2026_04_20_datasets.zip: dataset
    • 2026_04_20_top_view.zip: video from the drone camera at the top of the experiment

Experimental Setup & Hardware

The experimental architecture consists of four primary nodes—three stationary sensory buoys and one mobile ground-truth vessel.

  • Sensory Nodes (x3 USV Buoys):
    • Acoustic Sensors: Each buoy is equipped with an array of three H1a Hydrophones (Aquarian Audio).
    • Acquisition & Compute: Acoustic signals are processed via a HiFiBerry DAC+ ADC HAT connected to a Raspberry Pi.
    • Synchronization: A SmartMesh (LTC5800) wireless synchronization system is deployed across all nodes, providing a common time-span reference updating at a frequency of 2Hz.
  • Ground-Truth Node (x1 Vessel):
    • Vessel Specifications: A 4-meter boat powered by a petrol engine, acting as the primary acoustic source for validation and testing.
    • Spatial Tracking: Equipped with a RTK (Real-Time Kinematic) GPS module for high-precision localization.
    • Synchronization: Integrated with the same SmartMesh (LTC5800) system to perfectly align the boat's spatial data and engine noise events with the acoustic data captured by the sensory buoys.

Data Structure & Formats

The dataset provides raw and processed data streams across multiple standard formats to facilitate robotic framework integration and standalone signal processing.

  • ROS 2 Bags (.db3): Contains the complete serialized message streams for all sensor topics, allowing for full playback of the experimental trials within a Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2) environment.
  • CSV Files (.csv): Extracted tabular data for straightforward data analysis. These files contain:
    • High-precision RTK GPS positioning (Ground Truth).
    • SmartMesh synchronization timestamps (2Hz).
    • Corresponding ROS time (rostime).
    • Sensor telemetry and metadata.
  • Audio Files (.wav): Uncompressed, raw multi-channel acoustic data captured by the H1a hydrophone arrays, synced to the timeline established by the SmartMesh system.

/rms_stream/data

Hosts. Lamiaceae: * Agastache nepetoides (L.) Kuntze, [A. scrophulariifolia (Willd.) Kuntze], * Blephilia hirsuta (Pursh)Benth.,[Cantinoa mutabilis (Rich.)Harley&J.F.B.Pastore], Hedeoma hispida Pursh,[H.reverchonii (A.Gray) A.Gray, Lycopus americanus Muhl. ex W.P.C.Barton], L. *rubellus Moench, L. uniflorus Michx., L. *virginicus L., [Mentha aquatica L.], M. arvensis L., M. *canadensis L., [M. pulegium L., M. spicata L. (Eiseman & Blyth 2022), Monarda citriodora Cerv. ex Lag., M. clinopodia L.], M. didyma L., M. fistulosa L., M. *punctata L., [Monardella odoratissima Benth., Ocimum basilicum L., Origanum vulgare L. (Eiseman & Lonsdale 2022), Prunella vulgaris L., Pycnanthemum albescens Torr. & A.Gray, P. incanum (L.) Michx., P. muticum (Michx.) Pers., P. verticillatum var. pilosum (Nutt.) Cooperr.], * P. virginianum (L.) T.Durand & B.D.Jacks. ex B.L.Rob. & Fernald, [Scutellaria elliptica Muhl.], * S. integrifolia L. (Eiseman & Lonsdale 2018). The small, brownish blotch mine on Lycopus asper Greene reported from Alberta by Spencer (1969a) also seems likely to be the work of Calycomyza menthae. Hansson (1987) listed specimens of Chrysocharis crassiscapus (Eulophidae) reared in Alberta from Calycomyza on Agastache foeniculum (Pursh) Kuntze, Galeopsis tetrahit L., and Stachys palustris L., which may all represent C. menthae. Rearings from the latter two hosts in particular would be desirable, since the only Calycomyza -like mines we have seen on Galeopsis and Stachys appear actually to be the work of Nemorimyza posticata (Meigen) (rearing has confirmed both genera as hosts for this fly; see below).

Leaf mine. (Figs. 165, 166) On most hosts, the mine begins as a narrow brown line, expanding to a messy, brownish blotch with clean, whitish margins here and there; frass is in scattered grains and small lumps. Feeding lines like those of Nemorimyza posticata are visible in some examples. On Scutellaria, the mine begins as a gradually widening, serpentine track. On Blephilia, the mine is whitish and begins with a long, narrow linear portion with frass in black grains or strips along the sides, later expanding to a blotch that may obliterate the linear portion. Frass in the blotch may be in scattered black particles or almost nonexistent (Eiseman & Lonsdale 2018; Eiseman et al. 2019).

Puparium. Variously yellow, yellowish-brown, orange-brown, pale reddish-brown, brown, or dark brown; formed outside the mine.

Phenology and voltinism. This species is multivoltine. Larvae have been collected in late April (OK), late May (MA, NC), mid-June (MI, NC), early July (NC, OH, ON), mid-July (MA, IL, NC, ON), late July (NC, OR), early August (MA, OH), mid-August (MA), and mid-September (NC), with adults emerging 9–31 days later. Larvae collected in late August (NY, VT) and late September (MA) have overwintered as pupae, with adults emerging the following spring (Spencer 1969a; Eiseman & Lonsdale 2018).

Distribution. USA: [AL, CA, CT, DC, FL, GA, IA], *IL, [IN, KY, LA], MA, [MD, ME], *MI, [MN, MO], NC, [NE, NH, NJ, NM], *NY, *OH, OK, [OR, PA, TN, TX, VA], VT, [WI]; Canada: AB, [BC, MB, NB, NS], ON (Eiseman & Lonsdale 2018).

Comments. A female reared from Blephilia hirsuta was discussed by Eiseman & Lonsdale (2018) as Calycomyza sp. 4, and a male reared from Scutellaria integrifolia was misidentified by Eiseman et al. (2019) as C. novascotiensis Spencer (see discussion under C. novascotiensis below). With these now assigned to C. menthae, the only other Lamiaceae-feeding Calycomyza species known from the USA or Canada are C. hyptidis and C. salviae, sp. nov. Calycomyza hyptidis is a Neotropical species whose range extends into Florida (with leaf mines recently found in southeastern Georgia: iNat 256753539). It makes a distinctly different leaf mine, consisting of a star-shaped blotch with a mass of black frass accumulated in the center (Spencer & Stegmaier 1973). Calycomyza salviae is only known from California, on cultivated Salvia spp. that are not native to California. Its mine is not obviously different from that of C. menthae, which has not been confirmed to occur in California, and is only tentatively recorded there based on mines found on Monardella (Spencer 1981; Spencer & Steyskal 1986) and Ocimum (this paper), neither of which are confirmed hosts for C. menthae. The only other Calycomyza mines we have seen on Salvia have been in Texas (where C. menthae is recorded based on mines found on Monarda spp.), and we have tentatively assigned these to C. salviae.

metadata.title: Spatio-Temporal Dynamics of Bank Erosion and Accretion along the River Ganga in the Prayagraj Reach
metadata.description:
Riverbank erosion and sediment buildup (accretion) are fundamental fluvial mechanisms that drive channel shifts, floodplain development, and the long-term geomorphic evolution of alluvial systems. Due to its transitional meandering-to-braided pattern, erratic seasonal flow, heavy sediment volumes, and escalating human activity, the Prayagraj stretch of the Ganga River exhibits pronounced lateral instability. This study maps the spatial and temporal dynamics of bank erosion, accretion, and stable bank boundaries along this critical reach over a twenty-year span (2005–2025) by leveraging multi-temporal satellite data and GIS spatial mapping. By extracting the river channel outlines for 2005, 2015, and 2025, we used polygon overlay techniques to measure and track layout modifications across three distinct windows: 2005–2015, 2015–2025, and the full cumulative timeframe from 2005 to 2025.

 

The findings of this study demonstrate that bank erosion was the overriding geomorphic force across the two-decade study period, driving 54.79% of the total recorded bank alterations. Nonetheless, the timeline also highlights a notable shift: the heavily erosion-dominated behavior observed between 2005 and 2015 gave way to accelerated sediment accretion from 2015 to 2025, pointing to localized channel adjustments and increased deposition in recent years. Spatially, erosional losses were heavily clustered along downstream stretches and outer meander curves, whereas accretion was concentrated near inner bends, point bars, and cut-off channel segments. Ultimately, these trends underscore the persistent lateral volatility and active migration of the Ganga River within the Prayagraj reach. This research offers critical insights into long-term channel evolution, providing a solid scientific baseline to guide sustainable floodplain zoning, infrastructure safety, and targeted bank stabilization efforts throughout the region.

metadata.title: SEHAT- Healthcare & Medical Assistance Website
metadata.description:
Abstract

In recent years the internet is pretty much everywhere, in every house and on every phone. People use all kinds of sites to simplify things in daily life. This website is made based on an idea that could improve healthcare right in a local area. It would let users find out about hospitals nearby, or track down pharmacies and medical stores. There is also a way to get suggestions on which hospital might fit best, and even locate clinics easily, and BMI calculator to check body mass index. For users who struggle picking a hospital or booking a doctor appointment, this app seems like it could be useful. In emergencies, it might offer quick help too. Kind of bridges that gap when you need info fast. It is a handy helping tool which could revolutionize the healthcare system.

Keywords

Healthcare, Locality, Hospital Search, Appointment Booking, BMI Calculator
